DOUBLE-helix structure is a fundamental concept in molecular biology, representing the way DNA is organized. The discovery of the DOUBLE-helix by James Watson and Francis Crick in 1953 marked a significant transition in our understanding of genetics. This transition from a linear model to a DOUBLE-helix model changed how scientists view heredity and biological processes. 

The DOUBLE-helix consists of two strands that run in opposite directions, held together by hydrogen bonds between complementary nucleotide bases. This structure allows for accurate replication and transmission of genetic information. The DOUBLE-helix is essential for the storage and expression of genetic material.
